name: slack-bot-builder
description: Build Slack apps using the Bolt framework across Python, JavaScript, and Java. Covers Block Kit for rich UIs, interactive components, slash commands, event handling, OAuth installation flows, and Workflow Builder integration. Focus on best practices for production-ready Slack apps. Use when: slack bot, slack app, bolt framework, block kit, slash command.
capabilities: Create Bolt apps in Python, JavaScript, and Java with proper initialization
Build interactive Block Kit UIs with buttons, menus, and modals
Implement slash commands with proper acknowledgment patterns
Handle Slack events and message patterns with custom logic
Configure OAuth 2.0 flows for multi-workspace distribution
Design incident notification templates with action buttons
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The SKILL.md file is documentation containing educational code examples for the Slack Bolt framework. Environment variable usage follows security best practices for credential handling. No executable code with security risks was detected.
valueStatement: Building Slack integrations requires handling authentication, events, and complex UI patterns. This skill provides ready-to-use Bolt framework templates for Python, JavaScript, and Java that handle security, OAuth flows, and interactive Block Kit components.
userTitle: Build Slack Apps with Bolt Framework
limitations: Requires Slack app creation and token configuration in Slack API dashboard
Bolt framework dependencies must be installed separately for each language
Cannot bypass Slack API rate limits or platform restrictions
Production deployments require hosting infrastructure for HTTP endpoints

useCases.title: DevOps Incident Alerting
useCases.description: Create Slack bots that post formatted incident alerts with severity indicators and action buttons for acknowledging, escalating, or resolving incidents directly from Slack.

useCases.title: Team Notification Bot
useCases.description: Build bots that send automated notifications for CI/CD pipelines, code reviews, or deployment status with interactive elements for quick team responses.

useCases.title: Support Ticket System
useCases.description: Implement slash commands and modals for creating, tracking, and updating support tickets with custom fields and assignment workflows inside Slack.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Message Handler
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a Bolt app in Python that responds to messages containing specific keywords. Set up the app with environment variables for tokens, add a message listener for 'hello', and include a slash command that acknowledges the request and sends a follow-up message.
promptTemplates.scenario: Getting started with Bolt framework basics

promptTemplates.title: Interactive Button Component
promptTemplates.prompt: Build a Block Kit message with action buttons for an incident notification. Include a header with severity indicator, fields for incident details, and buttons for Acknowledge, Escalate, and Resolve actions. Handle button click events with appropriate callbacks.

promptTemplates.title: Modal Form Collection
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a slash command that opens a modal with input blocks for ticket title, description, and priority selection. Handle the view submission to extract form data and create a ticket confirmation message with the submitted values.
promptTemplates.scenario: Building forms for data collection in Slack

promptTemplates.title: OAuth Multi-Workspace App
promptTemplates.prompt: Set up a Bolt app with OAuth 2.0 configuration for multi-workspace deployment. Implement a database-backed installation store for persisting tokens, configure the required scopes minimally, and add the OAuth redirect routes for handling the installation flow.
promptTemplates.scenario: Preparing an app for public distribution

bestPractices: Request minimum required OAuth scopes to reduce user friction during installation and limit potential security exposure
Acknowledge all interactions within 3 seconds using ack() then process logic asynchronously to avoid timeout errors
Store tokens and secrets in environment variables or secure vaults, never hardcode or log credential values
antiPatterns: Hardcoding Slack tokens or signing secrets directly in source code instead of using environment variables
Requesting excessive OAuth scopes 'just in case' which increases security risk and reduces installation conversion
Processing events synchronously without acknowledgment which causes Slack to retry and duplicate events

faq.question: How do I create a new Slack app to use with this skill?
faq.answer: Visit https://api.slack.com/apps and click 'Create New App'. Choose 'From scratch' or 'From an app manifest'. After creation, navigate to 'OAuth & Permissions' to configure scopes and install the app to your workspace.

faq.question: Where do I find my Slack signing secret?
faq.answer: Go to your app settings at api.slack.com/apps, select 'Basic Information' from the left sidebar, and locate 'App Credentials'. Copy the 'Signing Secret' value and store it in your SLACK_SIGNING_SECRET environment variable.

faq.question: What is the difference between bot tokens and user tokens?
faq.answer: Bot tokens (xoxb-) represent your app and have scopes you configure. User tokens (xoxp-) represent individual users who install your app and inherit their permissions. Bot tokens are recommended for most automation scenarios.

faq.question: Do I need to host a public server to receive Slack events?
faq.answer: No. Use Socket Mode for development and testing, which receives events over WebSocket without public endpoints. For production, HTTP endpoints are more reliable but require a publicly accessible server with HTTPS.

faq.question: How do I test Block Kit layouts before implementing them?
faq.answer: Use the Block Kit Builder at app.slack.com/block-kit-builder to visually design and preview your message layouts. Export the JSON structure and integrate it into your Bolt app code.

faq.question: What happens if my app exceeds Slack API rate limits?
faq.answer: Slack returns HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header indicating seconds to wait. Implement exponential backoff retry logic in your app and consider batching operations or caching to reduce API calls.
